meta.name: ICG Inventory of Complicated Grief
meta.description: The ICG is a 19-item self-report measure developed by Prigerson et al. to assess the severity of complicated (prolonged) grief symptoms after bereavement.
meta.author: Prigerson et al. (1995)
meta.reference: Prigerson HG, Maciejewski PK, Reynolds CF, et al. Inventory of Complicated Grief: A scale to measure maladaptive symptoms of loss. Psychiatry Res. 1995;59(1-2):65-79.
meta.instruction: Please indicate how often you currently experience each of the following feelings.

questions.text: I think about this person so much that it's hard for me to do the things I normally do
Memories of the person who died upset me
I feel I cannot accept the death of the person who died
I feel myself longing for the person who died
I feel drawn to places and things associated with the person who died
I can't help feeling angry about the death
I feel disbelief over what happened
I feel stunned or dazed over what happened
Ever since the death, it is hard for me to trust people
Ever since the death, I feel like I have lost the ability to care about other people
I have pain in the same area of my body or have some of the same symptoms as the person who died
I go out of my way to avoid reminders that the person is gone
I feel that life is empty without the person who died
I hear the voice of the person who died speak to me
I see the person who died stand before me
I feel that it is unfair that I should live when this person died
I feel bitter over this person's death
I feel envious of others who have not lost someone close
I feel that the future holds no meaning or purpose without the person who died

ranges.label: Normal Grief Response
ranges.description: Your grief level is within the normal range. Sadness after losing a loved one is a natural emotional response. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Allow yourself to fully feel and express your grief
Maintain connections with friends and family for support
Take care of your basic daily needs
Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family

ranges.label: Grief Needing Attention
ranges.description: Your grief symptoms deserve attention. The grieving process varies for everyone, but if it continues to trouble you, consider seeking help. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Consider sharing your feelings with loved ones or a support group
Maintain the basic rhythm of daily life
Allow yourself to grieve in your own way and at your own pace
If distress persists beyond 6 months, consider consulting a professional
Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions

ranges.label: Possible Complicated Grief
ranges.description: Your score exceeds the screening threshold (>=30), suggesting possible complicated/prolonged grief disorder. Professional evaluation is recommended. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a professional counselor or psychiatrist
Learn about complicated grief/prolonged grief disorder
Grief-focused therapy (e.g., Complicated Grief Treatment) may help
Don't bear this alone — seek professional and social support
Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy

ranges.label: Severe Complicated Grief
ranges.description: You reported severe complicated grief symptoms that may significantly affect daily functioning and quality of life. Professional help is strongly recommended.
suggestions: Strongly recommend consulting a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist
Professional grief-focused therapy has been shown to be effective
Ensure you have a support system around you
If you have thoughts of self-harm, please seek help immediately
Combined medication and psychotherapy may be most effective
